pattern: A string of just two letters becomes 'balanced' (all of one letter before all of the other) once you pick a split point, so the answer is the minimum, over every possible split, of the letters that are on the wrong side of it -- which a single running tally can track without ever trying the splits one by one.
hints: Think of the final balanced string as some number of the first letter followed by some number of the second letter -- your job is to choose where that boundary falls and delete whatever doesn't fit.

Scan left to right while keeping a running count of the second letter seen so far. Whenever you hit the first letter, it either has to be deleted, or every second letter seen before it has to be deleted instead -- take whichever is cheaper so far.

Keep a running 'best cost so far' as you scan: each time you see the first letter, update it to the smaller of (best cost so far + 1) and (count of the second letter seen so far); each time you see the second letter, just increment its running count. The final running best cost is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one long run of b's in the middle
statement example, b's clustered at both ends
already perfectly balanced, no deletions needed
completely reversed, every character on the wrong side of any split
single b out of place at the very front
single a out of place at the very end
alternating characters, forces the running tally to update on every step

stress.description: random strings over just 'a' and 'b' at varying lengths and mix ratios
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    p = rng.random()
    s = ''.join('b' if rng.random() < p else 'a' for _ in range(n))
    return [s]

stress.description: long alternating and blocky patterns to stress the running-count logic
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 20000))
    block = rng.randint(1, 50)
    chars = []
    cur = rng.choice(['a', 'b'])
    count = 0
    for _ in range(n):
        chars.append(cur)
        count += 1
        if count >= block:
            cur = 'b' if cur == 'a' else 'a'
            count = 0
            block = rng.randint(1, 50)
    return [''.join(chars)]
